cases.query: How should I choose between rules, classic machine learning, LLM prompting, RAG, and agents for a new product requirement?

expected.heading: Choosing an Approach
expected.text: The first engineering decision is usually not which model — it is which class of solution:

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Reach for ML when the decision boundary is fuzzy, the signal is distributed across many weak features, or the rules would be brittle and expensive to maintain.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Evaluation is how you measure whether an LLM application is doing the right thing: answer quality, grounding, safety, and regressions over time.

cases.query: What are the main stages of a machine learning pipeline from data collection through monitoring?

expected.heading: Generic pipeline
expected.text: Data collection

expected.heading: Data Collection and Labeling
expected.text: Define the prediction target first, then collect inputs that are available at inference time and representative of production traffic.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: the real work is not the algorithm, it is building a reliable pipeline that is testable, deployable, and monitorable.

cases.query: Why is evaluation for LLM applications more layered than ordinary pass/fail testing, and what does it need to cover?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Because LLM output is probabilistic and open-ended, you cannot rely on a single pass/fail assertion the way you would for deterministic code

cases.query: What kinds of bugs can happen with C# value types versus reference types, and how can they be mitigated?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: A common source of bugs is value semantics versus reference semantics:

expected.heading: How It Works
expected.text: Repeated boxing in hot paths can create avoidable GC pressure.

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: Mitigate by preferring immutability for shared models or cloning at ownership boundaries.

cases.query: What should you consider when deciding what to include in an LLM's context window, and why is more context not always better?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Context engineering is the practice of deliberately deciding what goes into the model's context window

expected.heading: The Context Budget
expected.text: Everything competes for the same window

expected.heading: Techniques
expected.text: **Ordering and positioning.** Place the most important evidence at the start and end of the context to exploit primacy and recency; bury nothing critical in the middle.

cases.query: How do you manage a limited context budget when using retrieval, tools, and conversation history in an agentic system?

expected.heading: The Context Budget
expected.text: Everything competes for the same window

expected.heading: Techniques
expected.text: **Ordering and positioning.** Place the most important evidence at the start and end of the context to exploit primacy and recency; bury nothing critical in the middle.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Tools are the interface between an LLM's reasoning and the external world

cases.query: What techniques help improve the quality of assembled context for retrieval-augmented generation or long conversations?

expected.heading: Techniques
expected.text: Place the most important evidence at the start and end of the context

cases.query: How do prompt text, generation settings, and grounding work together to produce reliable model output?

expected.heading: LLM Settings
expected.text: Prompt text controls intent, while generation settings control sampling behavior and output boundaries.

expected.heading: Generation Parameters
expected.text: Generation parameters control how the model selects the next token from the predicted distribution.

expected.heading: Grounding and Citations
expected.text: Grounding constrains model output to evidence from provided context rather than parametric memory.

cases.query: How do embeddings and retrieval work together to match a natural-language query to the most relevant document chunks, even when there are no keyword overlaps?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Embeddings map text into a dense vector space where semantic similarity becomes geometric proximity

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Retrieval is the stage that decides what evidence enters the prompt.

expected.heading: Dense Retrieval — Vector Search
expected.text: An embedding model converts the query and each document chunk into fixed-size vectors in the same space.

cases.query: When should a RAG system use dense vector search, sparse keyword search, or both, and how are the results combined?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: The goal is to balance recall (find everything relevant), precision (exclude everything irrelevant), and latency across query types

expected.heading: Dense Retrieval — Vector Search
expected.text: How it works: - An embedding model converts the query and each document chunk into fixed-size vectors in the same space.

expected.heading: Sparse Retrieval — Keyword Search (BM25)
expected.text: BM25 is the same core idea — it is the algorithm behind those search indexes.

cases.query: What are the tradeoffs of using higher-dimensional embeddings, and how can Matryoshka training let you search faster without always using the full vector?

expected.heading: Model Families
expected.text: Three categories dominate production RAG systems: **Proprietary API models** — OpenAI `text-embedding-3-small` (1536-dim) and `text-embedding-3-large` (3072-dim) are the most widely deployed.

expected.heading: Dimensionality
expected.text: Higher dimensions give the model more room to represent fine-grained distinctions.

cases.query: What should a team monitor to detect retrieval regressions in a deployed RAG system, and which metrics would reveal coverage gaps or query-translation problems?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: RAG monitoring is the continuous observation of a deployed RAG pipeline to detect quality regressions, performance degradation, and data staleness before users notice.

expected.heading: Deterministic Metrics
expected.text: Even a small empty-result rate (>1%) signals coverage gaps in the index.

expected.heading: Instrumentation
expected.text: Each pipeline stage — query translation, embedding, retrieval, reranking, context assembly, generation — should emit its own span within a parent trace.

cases.query: What are the main stages of training a modern LLM, and how does each stage change what the model is good at?

expected.heading: How LLMs Are Built
expected.text: Training a modern LLM is a three-stage pipeline.

cases.query: When should you use prompting, retrieval-augmented generation, or fine-tuning to adapt an LLM to a task?

expected.heading: Adapting an LLM to a Task
expected.text: Three adaptation levers, ordered by cost.

cases.query: How do cosine similarity, dot product, and Euclidean distance differ for embeddings, and which is usually preferred for RAG?

expected.heading: Similarity Metrics
expected.text: The choice of similarity metric determines how "closeness" is calculated in vector space: **Cosine similarity** measures the angle between vectors, ignoring magnitude.

cases.query: Why can an embedding model swap break an existing vector index, and what should be done to avoid stale vectors?

expected.heading: Embedding Model Swap Invalidation
expected.text: Changing the embedding model — even a minor version — invalidates every stored vector.

cases.query: When should you choose fine-tuning instead of prompting or RAG for an LLM application?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: It is the most powerful and most expensive of the three adaptation levers

expected.heading: When to Fine-tune
expected.text: Reach for fine-tuning only after prompting and retrieval have been tried

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: It matters because knowledge changes faster than model weights

cases.query: What are the main tradeoffs between full fine-tuning and using a smaller model with distillation?

expected.heading: When to Fine-tune
expected.text: You want a smaller, cheaper model to match a larger one

expected.heading: Full Fine-tuning
expected.text: Update every weight in the model.

cases.query: Why is fine-tuning better than long prompts when prompt overhead is dominating cost?

expected.heading: When to Fine-tune
expected.text: Prompt overhead is dominating cost

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: without consuming context tokens

expected.heading: Dictionary
expected.text: Token counts determine context usage and API cost

cases.query: What kind of behavior is fine-tuning best suited to change, and what kinds of tasks are poor fits?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Fine-tuning continues training a pretrained model's weights on task-specific data, changing the model's behavior rather than just its inputs.

expected.heading: When to Fine-tune
expected.text: The behavior is hard to specify in words but easy to demonstrate

cases.query: What criteria should I use to choose a model for a production task, and how do latency, cost, and task difficulty affect that choice?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Model selection is choosing which model serves a request; routing is choosing per-request among several models.

expected.heading: Selection Criteria
expected.text: Choose a model against the actual requirements of the task, not its leaderboard rank:

cases.query: How does cascade routing work in a system that uses multiple models, and when should a request be escalated from a cheap model to a stronger one?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: The engineering job is to match each request to the cheapest model that can handle it, and to keep that mapping changeable as models and prices shift underneath you.

expected.heading: Routing Patterns
expected.text: Send the request to a cheap model first; escalate to a stronger one only when the cheap model fails a check

cases.query: What are the main stages of a RAG pipeline, and where do query translation, retrieval, reranking, context assembly, and groundedness checks fit?

expected.heading: Core Flow
expected.text: Each stage has its own page:

cases.query: Which production RAG pattern is the mainstream default, and how does it compare with query rewriting and routing or baseline single-pass RAG?

expected.heading: Choosing a Pattern
expected.text: The short version, in adoption order:

cases.query: How do intrinsic and extrinsic hallucinations differ, and which detection methods are best suited to each?

expected.heading: Intrinsic vs Extrinsic
expected.text: Ji et al.

expected.heading: Detection
expected.text: This is strong on intrinsic hallucinations where contradictions are explicit in context.

cases.query: What are the main root causes of hallucinations in LLMs, and how can temperature settings make them worse?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Three root causes show up repeatedly in production.

expected.heading: Temperature Miscalibration
expected.text: Setting temperature too high for factual tasks introduces token-level randomness that manifests as hallucinated details

cases.query: If a RAG system's answers seem unfaithful, how can you tell whether the problem is retrieval quality or generation quality?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: RAG evaluation decomposes into three layers: retrieval quality, generation quality, and end-to-end usefulness.

expected.heading: Retrieval Quality Metrics
expected.text: Retrieval quality metrics require a labeled evaluation set

expected.heading: LLM-as-Judge Metrics
expected.text: For semantic quality, run an LLM judge asynchronously on a sampled fraction of production traffic.

cases.query: What metrics and evaluation setup should be used to monitor a RAG pipeline over time?

expected.heading: Retrieval Quality Metrics
expected.text: These are computed on a scheduled basis (nightly or on every deployment) against a golden query set.

expected.heading: LLM-as-Judge Metrics
expected.text: Use binary pass/fail judgments rather than numeric scales

expected.heading: Performance and Cost Metrics
expected.text: Set SLOs on p95 end-to-end, but always diagnose regressions with per-stage breakdown.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Each layer has its own metrics, its own failure modes, and its own fix.

cases.query: When should I choose a Dictionary<TKey, TValue> instead of a List<T> or HashSet<T> in .NET?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: A data structure organizes data for efficient access, mutation, and iteration.

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: > [!QUESTION]- What is a data structure? Which ones do you know? Which of them exist in .NET? > A data structure is a way to organize related data into a collection-like object.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: `Dictionary<TKey, TValue>` is the primary key-value collection in .NET and the default choice for fast lookups by key in single-threaded or externally synchronized code.

cases.query: What are the main pitfalls of using Dictionary<TKey, TValue> with custom or mutable keys?

expected.heading: Pitfalls
expected.text: - **`GetHashCode`/`Equals` contract violation** — if `Equals` says two keys are equal but `GetHashCode` returns different values, the dictionary stores both as separate entries.

cases.query: How do data structure choice and algorithm choice interact when optimizing .NET code?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: choosing the right collection, avoiding quadratic blowups in batch processing, and reasoning about tradeoffs before writing code.

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: Start with the data structure.

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Choosing the right collection usually has a bigger impact on performance than micro-optimizing the code that uses it.

cases.query: How are graphs represented in .NET, and what collections are commonly used to model them?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Graphs model relationships between entities using vertices (nodes) and edges.

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: Examples include arrays, lists, queues, stacks, linked lists, dictionaries/hash tables, hash sets, graphs, and trees.

cases.query: How do prompt injection attacks differ between direct, indirect, and multimodal forms in LLM applications, and why do retrieval pathways increase the risk?

expected.heading: Prompt Injection (LLM01)
expected.citationLabel: 11 AI & ML/LLM/OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM.md
expected.text: **Mechanism**: The model receives attacker instructions in the same natural-language channel as legitimate instructions, so it may follow malicious text even when system guidance says not to.

cases.query: What are the main OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications vulnerabilities in the 2025 edition, and which new items were added compared with the classic list?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.citationLabel: 11 AI & ML/LLM/OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM.md
expected.text: The OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications (2025 edition) catalogs the highest-impact security failures in systems that integrate large language models.

expected.heading: The 2025 List
expected.citationLabel: 11 AI & ML/LLM/OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM.md
expected.text: LLM01 | Prompt Injection | Attacker-crafted input overrides system instructions

cases.query: How should a team decide between RAG and fine-tuning when knowledge changes frequently versus when the problem is behavioral?

expected.heading: RAG vs Fine-Tuning
expected.text: RAG externalizes knowledge into retrievable sources, while fine-tuning changes model behavior in weights.

expected.heading: Questions
expected.text: When the failure is behavioral, not factual: the model retrieves the right evidence but keeps producing the wrong format, tone, or policy behavior despite prompt iteration.

cases.query: What layered guardrails are recommended to reduce prompt injection risk and keep LLM systems bounded in production?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Guardrails are layered controls around an LLM that reduce risk: they prevent unsafe actions, limit data exposure, and keep outputs within policy and quality constraints.

expected.heading: Defense-in-Depth Model
expected.text: ```mermaid flowchart TD U[User Input] --> IG[Input Guardrails] IG -->|blocked| R1[Reject / Rephrase] IG -->|allowed| CG[Context Guardrails] CG --> LLM[LLM] LLM --> OG[Output Guardrails] OG -->|blocked

expected.heading: Input Guardrails
expected.text: Validate and filter what reaches the LLM: - **Prompt injection detection** — identify attempts to override system instructions ("Ignore all previous instructions and...").

cases.query: Why can adding more retrieved documents or a longer prompt make answer quality worse, and what should you do instead?

expected.heading: More Context, Worse Answers
expected.text: attention dilutes across a larger window (context rot), and critical evidence lands in the low-attention middle (lost in the middle).

expected.heading: Lost-in-the-Middle Attention Failure
expected.text: When relevant evidence lands in the middle of a long context, the model underweights it relative to evidence at the start or end.

cases.query: How should a RAG system be designed to reduce hallucinations and keep answers grounded after retrieval?

expected.heading: Intro
expected.text: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) combines retrieval and generation: retrieve evidence from your corpus, then generate an answer grounded in that evidence.

expected.heading: RAG Does Not Eliminate Hallucinations
expected.text: RAG introduces its own failure modes: retrieval miss, context overflow, and model additions beyond retrieved evidence.

cases.query: What should a system do when retrieved sources disagree, instead of silently choosing one version?

expected.heading: Grounding Bypass Under Conflicting Evidence
expected.text: When retrieved sources contain conflicting information, models often silently pick one version instead of flagging the conflict.

cases.query: How can you reduce the risk that a model returns a schema-valid JSON response with the wrong semantic values?

expected.heading: Structured Output Schema Mismatch
expected.text: The model produces valid JSON that passes schema validation but contains semantically wrong values — wrong field mappings, hallucinated enum values that happen to be valid strings, or arrays with the
